analogy: Think of "Human Reproduction" through the chapter's own opening images: You have learnt about human reproductive system and its functions in Chapter 2. Now, let’s discuss a closely related topic – reproductive health. The term simply refers to healthy reproductive organs with normal functions. However, it has a broader perspective and includes the emotional and... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Human Reproduction is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that you have learnt about human reproductive system and its functions in Chapter 2.

The text explains that now, let’s discuss a closely related topic – reproductive health.

The text explains that the term simply refers to healthy reproductive organs with normal functions.

The text explains that however, it has a broader perspective and includes the emotional and social aspects of reproduction also.

The text explains that according to the World Health Organisation (WHO), reproductive health means a total well-being in all aspects of reproduction, i.e., physical, emotional, behavioural and social.

The text explains that therefore, a society with people having physically and functionally normal reproductive organs and normal emotional and behavioural interactions among them in all sex-related aspects might be called reproductively healthy.

The text explains that why is it significant to maintain reproductive health and what are the methods taken up to achieve it?

The text explains that 3.1 REPRODUCTIVE HEALTH – PROBLEMS AND STRATEGIES India was amongst the first countries in the world to initiate action plans and programmes at a national level to attain total reproductive health as a social goal.

The text explains that these programmes called ‘family planning’ were initiated in 1951 and were periodically assessed over the past decades.

The text explains that improved programmes covering wider CHAPTER 3 REPRODUCTIVE HEALTH 3.1 Reproductive Health – Problems and Strategies 3.2 Population Explosion and Birth Control 3.3 Medical Termination of Pregnancy 3.4 Sexually Transmitted Diseases 3.5 Infertility

The text explains that 42 BIOLOGY reproduction-related areas are currently in operation under the popular name ‘Reproductive and Child Health Care (RCH) programmes’.

The text explains that creating awareness among people about various reproduction related aspects and providing facilities and support for building up a reproductively healthy society are the major tasks under these programmes.

The text explains that with the help of audio-visual and the print-media governmental and non-governmental agencies have taken various steps to create awareness among the people about reproduction-related aspects.

The text explains that parents, other close relatives, teachers and friends, also have a major role in the dissemination of the above information.

The text explains that introduction of sex education in schools should also be encouraged to provide right information to the young so as to discourage children from believing in myths and having misconceptions about sex-related aspects.

The text explains that proper information about reproductive organs, adolescence and related changes, safe and hygienic sexual practices, sexually transmitted diseases (STD), AIDS, etc., would help people, especially those in the adolescent age group to lead a reproductively healthy life.

The text explains that the world population which was around 2 billion (2000 million) in 1900 rocketed to about 6 billion by 2000 and 7.2 billion in 2011.

The text explains that intra cytoplasmic sperm injection (ICSI) is another specialised procedure to form an embryo in the laboratory in which a sperm is directly injected into the ovum.

The text explains that suggest some methods to assist infertile couples to have children.

The text explains that what are the measures one has to take to prevent from contracting STDs?

The text explains that state True/False with explanation (a) Abortions could happen spontaneously too.

The text explains that (True/False) (b) Infertility is defined as the inability to produce a viable offspring and is always due to abnormalities/defects in the female partner.

The text explains that (True/False) (c) Complete lactation could help as a natural method of contraception.

The text explains that (True/False) (d) Creating awareness about sex related aspects is an effective method to improve reproductive health of the people.

The text explains that correct the following statements : (a) Surgical methods of contraception prevent gamete formation.

The text explains that (b) All sexually transmitted diseases are completely curable.

The text explains that (c) Oral pills are very popular contraceptives among the rural women.

The text explains that t. techniques, embryos are always transferred into the uterus.

Source: NCERT Class 12 Biology, Chapter 3: Human Reproduction

schemes.name: According
schemes.point: According to the World Health Organisation (WHO), reproductive health means a total well-being in all aspects of reproduction, i.e., physical, emotional, behavioural and social. (NCERT Class 12 Biology Ch3 pg 1)

schemes.name: Through
schemes.point: Through our Reproductive Child Health (RCH) programme, though we could bring down the population growth rate, it was only marginal. (NCERT Class 12 Biology Ch3 pg 2)

schemes.name: Therefore
schemes.point: Therefore, the government was forced to take up serious measures to check this population growth rate. (NCERT Class 12 Biology Ch3 pg 2)

schemes.name: It
schemes.point: It also should in no way interfere with the sexual drive, desire and/or the sexual act of the user. (NCERT Class 12 Biology Ch3 pg 2)

schemes.name: Government
schemes.point: Government of India legalised MTP in 1971 with some strict conditions to avoid its misuse. (NCERT Class 12 Biology Ch3 pg 5)

prelims_traps: Do not treat 3.1 REPRODUCTIVE HEALTH – PROBLEMS AND and STRATEGIES as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Human Reproduction; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Human Reproduction through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 3.1 REPRODUCTIVE HEALTH – PROBLEMS AND and STRATEGIES to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
